items.q: How are bacteria able to make human insulin?
items.correct: The human insulin gene is inserted into the bacteria's DNA (genetic modification), so they transcribe and translate it to make insulin.
items.misconception: Bacteria naturally produce human insulin in their own bodies.
items.random_wrong: Bacteria are fed human insulin and then release it again.
items.why: Students miss that the bacteria are genetically modified to carry the human gene.

items.q: What gas makes bread dough rise?
items.correct: Yeast respires anaerobically and produces carbon dioxide, which forms bubbles that make the dough rise.
items.misconception: Bread rises because yeast produces oxygen.
items.random_wrong: Bread rises only because oven heat turns water to steam.
items.why: Students confuse the gas of fermentation with oxygen rather than carbon dioxide.

items.q: Does selective breeding change DNA the way genetic modification does?
items.correct: No - selective breeding chooses existing parents over generations; it does not directly insert genes from another species.
items.misconception: Selective breeding means adding new genes into an organism in a lab.
items.random_wrong: Selective breeding only works on plants, never animals.
items.why: Students blur the line between choosing parents and laboratory gene transfer.

items.q: Bacteria can be used to make human insulin because they have been:
items.correct: genetically modified with the human insulin gene
items.why: It's genetically modified with the human insulin gene. “fed pure human insulin” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: In bread-making, yeast makes dough rise by producing:
items.why: Correct answer: carbon dioxide. “oxygen” sounds right because it borrows the language of the topic — but it fails this question. Re-read the notes on why.

items.q: Selective breeding increases a desired feature by:
items.correct: breeding individuals that show that feature over many generations
items.misconception: inserting new genes in a laboratory
items.random_wrong: raising the temperature of the eggs
items.why: It's breeding individuals that show that feature over many generations. “inserting new genes in a laboratory” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.

items.q: Raising CO2 levels in a glasshouse increases crop yield because it:
items.why: It's increases the rate of photosynthesis. “kills all the pests present” is the classic near-miss here; knowing why it's wrong is worth as much as knowing the right answer.
